sections.text: Welcome to AP Biology, Module Fourteen — Ecosystems and Energy Flow. This is still College Board Unit Eight, Ecology. Last time we looked at how species interact. Today we follow energy from the sun all the way up a food chain — and discover why it can only travel in one direction, while matter cycles around forever.

Here's a puzzle. An acre of grain feeds far more people than an acre raising beef. The very same rule explains why apex predators — lions, sharks, eagles — are so rare. It all comes down to how much energy gets lost at every step of a food chain. By the end of today, you'll know the rule and the math behind it.

Here's our plan. First, energy flow and the famous ten percent rule — and why energy and matter behave so differently. Then productivity and ecological pyramids, including GPP versus NPP and which pyramids can flip upside down. Finally, the carbon and nitrogen cycles — the exact routes atoms take as they get recycled.

Every ecosystem is built in tiers called trophic levels. At the bottom, producers, or autotrophs, fix their own energy through photosynthesis — plants, algae, cyanobacteria. Above them, primary consumers eat producers, secondary consumers eat herbivores, and tertiary consumers sit on top. Decomposers — bacteria and fungi — sit alongside all of them, recycling dead matter and waste at every single level, not just at the end.

Here's the single most important idea in this unit, and a classic AP trap. Energy flows one way — sun, to producers, to consumers, to decomposers, and finally out as heat. It is never recycled, so the sun has to keep resupplying it. Matter, by contrast, cycles endlessly — carbon and nitrogen atoms get reused over and over. Saying energy returns to producers will cost you the point.

Now the ten percent rule, also called ecological efficiency. Only about ten percent of the energy at one level gets built into the next level's bodies. Where does the other ninety percent go? Lost as heat from cellular respiration, lost in undigested material like feces, and lost in parts that never get eaten. That huge loss is exactly why food chains almost never go beyond four or five links.

Let's put numbers on it. Start with ten thousand kilocalories at the producer level — and notice that's NPP, the energy left after producers respire, so it's what consumers can actually access. Multiply by ten percent at each step: one thousand, then one hundred, then just ten kilocalories at the top. Out of ten thousand, only ten reach the apex predator. That's the math behind our grain-versus-beef puzzle.

Your turn. Producers in a meadow capture fifty thousand kilocalories. Using the ten percent rule, how much energy is available to the secondary consumers — that's the third trophic level? Work it step by step. Pause now. Solve it. Press play when you're ready.

Here's the solution. Fifty thousand times ten percent gives five thousand kilocalories for the primary consumers. Then ten percent again gives five hundred kilocalories for the secondary consumers. Two steps up means multiplying by ten percent twice — that's times zero-point-zero-one. The answer is five hundred kilocalories.

Productivity comes in two flavors. GPP, gross primary productivity, is the total energy producers fix from sunlight. But producers respire too, so we subtract that: NPP equals GPP minus producer respiration. NPP is the energy actually left over for consumers. It's highest in tropical rainforests, coral reefs, and estuaries, and lowest in deserts and the deep ocean.

Don't mix these two up. GPP is the gross total fixed — the bigger number — and it includes the energy producers will burn for their own respiration. NPP is what remains after producers pay that respiration bill — the smaller number, stored as new biomass. When a question asks what's available to consumers, the answer is always NPP. Remember to subtract producer respiration.

We stack ecosystems three ways. The energy pyramid is always upright — the ten percent rule guarantees less energy each level up. Biomass pyramids can flip in the ocean: biomass is a snapshot of mass at one instant. Phytoplankton reproduce and get eaten so fast that little mass exists at any moment, yet their high turnover still supplies more energy over time — so energy stays upright even when biomass inverts. Number pyramids flip too — one tree feeds thousands of insects.

Carbon is the backbone of life, and it cycles constantly. Photosynthesis pulls CO2 from the air into glucose; respiration by consumers and decomposers sends it back. Some carbon gets locked away for ages — in fossil fuels, in limestone, and dissolved in the ocean. When we burn fossil fuels, we release that ancient carbon, pushing atmospheric CO2 from about two hundred eighty parts per million before industry to over four hundred twenty today — driving warming and ocean acidification.

Nitrogen is everywhere in the air — seventy-eight percent — but as inert N2 most life can't touch it. Five steps make it usable. Fixation turns N2 into ammonia, done by Rhizobium and cyanobacteria. Nitrification converts ammonium to nitrate, by Nitrosomonas then Nitrobacter. Plants assimilate that nitrogen into amino acids and nucleic acids. Decomposers ammonify dead matter back to ammonium. And denitrifying bacteria return nitrogen to the air as N2.

One nitrogen term trips everyone up. Fixation means one precise thing: taking inert atmospheric N2 and turning it into ammonia — done by Rhizobium, cyanobacteria, or industrially by Haber-Bosch. Nitrification, assimilation, ammonification, and denitrification are all different steps. If it doesn't begin with N2 gas and end at ammonia, it is not fixation.

Let's lock it in. Energy flows one way and exits as heat; matter cycles. Ten percent moves up, ninety percent is lost — so food chains stay short. NPP equals GPP minus producer respiration, and consumers eat NPP. The energy pyramid is always upright, but biomass and numbers pyramids can invert. Carbon: photosynthesis fixes it, respiration releases it, and fossil fuel burning spiked CO2 past four hundred twenty parts per million. Nitrogen: fixation, nitrification, assimilation, ammonification, denitrification — and fixation means N2 to ammonia only.

Nicely done — you've built the foundation. To master it, read the OpenStax Biology chapters on energy flow and biogeochemical cycles, then run the Khan Academy AP Biology Unit Eight problem sets on the ten percent rule and the nitrogen cycle. Now open your GIIS dashboard and complete the Module Fourteen energy-budget assignment. The trophic levels and ten percent rule you just learned are exactly what Module Fifteen reuses for biomagnification and dead zones. See you there.
